FHFA county limit and the lender's written loan options
Understand which financing categories may be reviewed
Which costs are included in projected payments?
Loan Estimate and, before closing, Closing Disclosure
Separate principal and interest, escrow, mortgage insurance, and cash to close
table.note: State tools and federal limits organize the questions; written property and loan records supply the transaction inputs.

sections.heading: Place a higher loan amount in county context
paragraphs: For 2026, FHFA set the one-unit baseline conforming loan limit at $832,750 and the one-unit high-cost-area ceiling at $1,249,125. The actual conforming limit is county-specific. Some California counties use the baseline, while qualifying high-cost areas use a higher amount up to the ceiling. The applicable figure should be checked against FHFA's county list for the year of the loan.

A conforming limit describes the maximum loan size Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac may acquire under that framework; it is not a mortgage offer and does not show whether a particular borrower or property fits a product. Compare loan amount, APR, interest rate, points, lender fees, reserves, mortgage insurance when applicable, and total cash to close using written disclosures. A smaller down payment can preserve cash for taxes or insurance, but it can also change the loan amount and other costs, so test the tradeoff rather than optimizing one line in isolation.

ctaBreaks.heading: Model the recurring payment and the first-year cash separately
ctaBreaks.body: Use the payment calculator for the monthly scenario, then keep supplemental taxes and initial insurance costs in a separate closing-year worksheet.
ctaBreaks.label: Build a California payment scenario

ctaBreaks.heading: Compare California markets before narrowing the property
ctaBreaks.body: Open the statewide location page for local tax, insurance, price, and mortgage-planning links across California cities.
ctaBreaks.label: Explore California mortgage markets

ctaBreaks.heading: Review mortgage options with the county limit in mind
ctaBreaks.body: Use the rates page to compare available structures, then refine the property and borrower details before choosing an option.
ctaBreaks.label: Compare mortgage options

articles.conclusion: California planning works best as four linked reviews: the new assessment, any supplemental bill, the actual insurance contract, and the county's current loan limit. None can be inferred safely from a listing price or the seller's payment. Gather those records early, keep recurring expenses separate from one-time cash needs, and update the comparison when a county notice, insurer, or written loan term changes.

faqs.question: Can I use the seller's California property-tax bill for my mortgage budget?
faqs.answer: Use it as background, not as the buyer's final estimate. A change in ownership generally creates a new base-year value, and local bonded debt, assessments, exemptions, and supplemental billing can affect the buyer's costs.

faqs.question: Is a California FAIR Plan policy the same as a standard homeowners policy?
faqs.answer: No. California's Department of Insurance describes the FAIR Plan as a last-resort option with a basic fire policy and coverage limitations. A separate difference-in-conditions policy may be considered for some gaps, subject to availability and terms.

faqs.question: Does the 2026 high-cost conforming ceiling apply everywhere in California?
faqs.answer: No. FHFA publishes county-specific limits. The national ceiling is the maximum for qualifying high-cost areas, while other counties may use the baseline or another amount shown in FHFA's list.

sourceClaims.claim: Proposition 13 generally limits the rate to 1 percent plus voter-approved bonded debt and establishes an acquisition-based assessment framework.
A change in ownership may create one or two prorated supplemental bills in addition to the annual bill, and the bill is sent to the owner rather than copied to the lender.
California consumers should compare policy terms and exclusions; flood and earthquake are among the losses generally excluded from standard homeowners coverage.
The FAIR Plan is a last-resort basic property option with limitations, and a difference-in-conditions policy may be considered for some uncovered perils.
The 2026 one-unit baseline is $832,750, the high-cost ceiling is $1,249,125, and the applicable conforming limit varies by county.
The Loan Estimate separates projected payments, estimated taxes and insurance, closing costs, and cash-to-close information for comparison.

articles.title: Colorado property tax and insurance considerations for buyers
articles.summary: Colorado buyers can create a stronger housing budget by tracing the local tax calculation and comparing property-specific insurance coverage before relying on a listing estimate.
articles.dek: Actual value, statutory assessment rules, local mill levies, wildfire or hail exposure, roof terms, and deductibles can all change the cost of owning two similarly priced homes.
articles.metaDescription: Understand Colorado property-tax calculations, local mill levies, homeowners insurance terms, and escrow planning before buying a home.
introduction: A Colorado home's purchase price does not translate directly into one statewide property-tax rate. The county establishes actual value, state law determines how value is converted for assessment, and local jurisdictions apply mill levies. Recent legislation has also used different valuation rules for school-district and other local-government levies, making old shortcuts especially unreliable.

Insurance requires a second address-level review. Colorado's Division of Insurance points consumers to coverage summaries, comparison resources, wildfire and hail information, and a homeowners and HOA toolkit. The guidance below is current through July 13, 2026, but an assessor record, adopted mill levies, and a written insurance offer are needed to turn the framework into a property budget.
keyTakeaways: Trace the calculation from actual value to assessed value and then through every applicable mill levy instead of using a statewide effective-rate shortcut.
Verify the property-tax year and current state adjustments because Colorado assessment formulas can change and may differ between school and non-school levies.
Compare homeowners policies by coverage, valuation method, roof treatment, deductibles, exclusions, and additional-living-expense terms as well as premium.
For a condominium or planned community, review both the association's master policy and the owner's proposed coverage before estimating the full monthly obligation.

sections.heading: Trace value, assessment, and mill levies
paragraphs: Colorado property tax begins with actual value. An assessment formula set under state law converts that value into assessed value, and the applicable local mill levies are then applied. One mill represents one dollar of tax for each $1,000 of assessed value. The parcel may be served by a county, municipality, school district, fire district, metropolitan district, or other local entities, so the combined levy depends on location.

For recent tax years, state law has distinguished between valuation used for school-district levies and valuation used for other local governments. That means a single assessment percentage may not reproduce the complete bill. Obtain the county assessor's property record and the current tax statement, then identify the property-tax year, actual value, adjustments, assessed values, and mill levies shown for that parcel.

sections.heading: Use the right tax year and local record
paragraphs: A listing may display the most recent tax paid, but that amount can reflect a prior valuation cycle, a different set of levies, or a classification that needs review. Ask the assessor which value period supports the current notice and whether a sale changes any assumptions in the estimate. For the payment worksheet, preserve the assessor's annual figure as one scenario and add a higher planning case if the next valuation or levy is not yet known.

Tax relief programs have their own conditions and application processes. Do not reduce the budget for an exemption, classification, rebate, or deferral until the administering agency confirms how it applies. If a notice appears incorrect, follow the appeal instructions and deadline on the notice rather than waiting for the mortgage servicer's next escrow analysis.

sections.heading: Compare the insurance terms that carry loss risk
paragraphs: Colorado insurers must provide summary information that explains major coverages and exclusions, but the Division of Insurance emphasizes that the summary does not replace the policy. Review the declarations and contract for dwelling coverage, other structures, personal property, additional living expense, liability, special limits, exclusions, and endorsements. Then compare whether losses are settled using replacement cost or actual cash value and when depreciation may apply.

Wildfire, hail, roof condition, construction features, and location can affect underwriting, terms, and premium. Ask each insurer how the roof will be valued after a covered loss, whether a wind or hail deductible differs from the standard deductible, what mitigation information it considers, and whether the dwelling limit reflects current reconstruction costs. The answer must come from the offered policy, not a general market description.

table.caption: Colorado property budget document check
rows: Property valuation
County assessor record, notice of value, and property classification
Choose the actual-value input and identify the governing tax year
Assessment and levies
Current tax statement showing assessed values and mill levies
Estimate annual taxes for the exact parcel
Dwelling and roof coverage
Insurance quote, declarations, policy form, roof schedule, and endorsements
Compare premium with the amount and method of potential claim payment
Catastrophe deductibles
Dollar amount or percentage for wind, hail, wildfire, and other applicable perils
Set a cash-reserve target outside the monthly premium
Association insurance
HOA master policy, deductible, loss-assessment terms, and proposed owner policy
Identify coverage gaps and costs not shown in principal and interest
table.note: Policy availability, deductible acceptability, and loan requirements depend on the property, insurer, association documents, and lender review.

sections.heading: Connect annual costs to the total payment
paragraphs: Principal and interest are only part of the monthly housing obligation. Taxes and homeowners insurance may be collected through escrow, while association dues and some owner-policy costs may be paid separately. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau recommends reviewing the total monthly payment and notes that an escrow amount can change when tax or insurance bills change.

Use an annual-to-monthly conversion for comparison, then retain the annual bills and deductible exposure as separate cash planning lines. A lower premium paired with a larger percentage deductible may reduce the recurring amount while increasing what the homeowner must fund after a covered loss. Neither choice is automatically preferable; compare it with available reserves, the home's risk features, and the written policy.

ctaBreaks.heading: Start with the Colorado market, then narrow to the parcel
ctaBreaks.body: Use the state location page to reach Denver, Colorado Springs, Boulder, and other city planning pages before gathering address-level records.
ctaBreaks.label: Explore Colorado mortgage markets

ctaBreaks.heading: See how taxes and insurance move the monthly total
ctaBreaks.body: Enter annual estimates separately so the calculator shows which housing-cost input is changing the scenario.
ctaBreaks.label: Calculate a total payment scenario

articles.conclusion: Colorado's tax and insurance costs become manageable when the source documents are read in sequence. Start with the assessor and current levies, then read the actual insurance offer and any association master policy. Convert the recurring amounts for monthly comparison without losing sight of deductibles, coverage gaps, and future escrow adjustments that may require cash beyond the scheduled principal and interest.

faqs.question: How is Colorado property tax generally calculated?
faqs.answer: The general chain is actual value multiplied by the applicable assessment formula to determine assessed value, followed by the local mill levies. Current adjustments and the set of taxing districts must be verified for the parcel and tax year.

faqs.question: Does a homeowners premium comparison show which Colorado policy is best for a home?
faqs.answer: No. A comparison can help organize shopping, but the offered premium, coverages, exclusions, valuation method, deductibles, and property underwriting determine the actual option. Review the full written policy terms.

faqs.question: Can taxes or insurance change a fixed-rate mortgage payment?
faqs.answer: They can change the total amount paid to the servicer when those costs are escrowed. A fixed interest rate does not freeze property taxes, insurance premiums, association dues, or other ownership expenses.

sourceClaims.claim: Colorado's Division of Property Taxation oversees statewide property-tax administration and directs taxpayers to valuation, exemption, and appeal resources.
Colorado's general calculation applies an assessment rate to actual value and then applies local mill levies to assessed value.
SB24-233 established distinct valuation rules for school-district and other local-government levies, subject to later amendments that also govern the 2026 property-tax year.
HB24B-1001 amended Colorado's recent residential valuation rules and established the school-district limit used with the 2026 tax-year framework.
Colorado Legislative Council Staff reported that no balancing reduction below the permanent 7.05% school-district residential assessment rate was required for property-tax year 2026.
Colorado requires summary disclosures for major coverages and exclusions, while the actual policy controls the contract terms.
Colorado's consumer toolkit identifies current homeowners and HOA insurance issues, shopping steps, mitigation questions, and policy-review considerations.
A total monthly mortgage payment may include taxes and insurance, and an escrow amount can change even when scheduled principal and interest do not.

articles.title: Florida insurance costs and mortgage payment planning
articles.summary: Florida homeowners insurance belongs near the start of mortgage planning because the premium, coverage limits, hurricane deductible, and flood protection are specific to the property.
articles.dek: A complete comparison looks beyond the annual premium to the policy's wind terms, valuation method, exclusions, separate flood coverage, and cash needed before insurance responds to a covered loss.
articles.metaDescription: Review Florida homeowners insurance, hurricane deductibles, flood coverage, escrow, and cash-reserve questions before estimating a mortgage payment.
introduction: In Florida, the same purchase price can lead to very different insurance results based on location, construction, roof details, insured value, selected coverage, deductibles, and the insurer's review. That makes a listing's premium history a weak substitute for a current quote in the buyer's name. The insurance conversation should begin while the property and financing terms can still be evaluated together.

The Florida Department of Financial Services provides consumer guidance on homeowners coverage and hurricane deductibles, while FEMA explains that most homeowners policies do not cover flood damage. These sources describe how to ask better questions, not what a particular policy will cost. The state and federal information in this guide was reviewed on July 13, 2026.
keyTakeaways: Request property-specific insurance terms early and compare the declarations, policy form, endorsements, exclusions, valuation basis, and deductibles before relying on the premium.
Translate a percentage hurricane deductible into dollars using the policy's dwelling limit, not the home's purchase price or the mortgage balance.
Treat wind coverage and flood coverage as separate questions because Florida's hurricane coverage definition does not include flooding and standard homeowners policies generally exclude flood loss.
Budget both the recurring premium and the cash exposure under deductibles; escrow can spread premiums monthly but does not fund the homeowner's share of a claim.

sections.heading: Read the policy from coverage to exclusions
paragraphs: Start with the written quote and declarations page. Identify the dwelling limit, other structures, personal property, loss-of-use or additional-living-expense coverage, liability, and every listed deductible. Then read the policy form and endorsements for exclusions, roof settlement terms, water limitations, wind treatment, and whether covered property is valued at replacement cost or actual cash value. A premium comparison without these terms can place unlike policies side by side.

Florida's consumer guidance notes that a lender may require homeowners insurance when a mortgage is attached to the property. That requirement protects the lender's interest, but it does not mean every peril or every rebuilding expense is covered. Ask the insurer and lender to address questions in writing, especially when a deductible is stated as a percentage or when wind coverage is handled separately.

sections.heading: Translate the hurricane deductible into cash
paragraphs: Florida law defines when the hurricane deductible applies and how it operates across covered hurricane losses during a calendar year. The Department of Financial Services explains that available deductible options and exceptions depend on dwelling limits and policy type. The declarations page must show the deductible as a dollar amount even when the contract also expresses it as a percentage.

A 2 percent hurricane deductible on a $450,000 dwelling limit equals $9,000. The actual dollar amount, covered event, remaining calendar-year deductible, and claim treatment come from the policy and Florida rules. Keep the deductible in a liquid-reserve discussion rather than dividing it across the monthly payment as though it were a premium.

sections.heading: Separate wind damage from flood damage
paragraphs: Florida's hurricane coverage guidance addresses windstorm loss during a hurricane and expressly states that flooding is not included. FEMA similarly advises that most homeowners and renters policies do not cover flood damage. A home outside a mapped high-risk area can still have flood exposure, and a lender's decision not to require flood insurance does not establish that the property cannot flood.

Ask for the property's current flood-zone information, an elevation certificate when relevant and available, and written flood-insurance options. Compare building and contents coverage, deductibles, waiting periods, exclusions, and effective dates. Keep the homeowners and flood policies together in the budget so one premium is not mistaken for complete hazard protection.

table.caption: Florida insurance and mortgage review
rows: What does the homeowners policy cover?
Quote, declarations, policy form, endorsements, and coverage summary
Use the premium only after comparing limits and exclusions
How large is the hurricane deductible in dollars?
Declarations page and the dwelling limit used in the percentage calculation
Hold the exposure as a separate cash-reserve target
Is wind included, limited, or written separately?
Wind coverage terms and any separate policy or exclusion
Combine all required premiums for the recurring estimate
What flood protection is available?
Flood-zone record and a separate NFIP or private flood quote
Add premium and deductible without assuming homeowners coverage applies
How will the mortgage handle premiums?
Loan Estimate, Closing Disclosure, and escrow instructions
Distinguish monthly escrow from upfront premium and prepaid amounts
table.note: Insurance terms and lender requirements vary. Confirm the offered policies and financing conditions for the specific property.

sections.heading: Build a payment plan and a loss-reserve plan
paragraphs: When insurance is escrowed, the servicer generally collects part of the expected annual premium with each mortgage payment and pays the bill when due. A renewal change can alter the escrow projection and total monthly amount. Review the first-year projected payment on the Loan Estimate, then compare it with the final Closing Disclosure and the insurer's invoice so the same premium is not omitted or counted twice.

The monthly worksheet should sit beside a reserve worksheet. The first tracks principal, interest, property taxes, homeowners insurance, flood insurance, mortgage insurance when applicable, and association dues. The second tracks hurricane, wind, flood, roof, water, and other deductibles plus likely maintenance needs. Florida's Office of Insurance Regulation publishes county and ZIP-level market data reported by insurers, but the Office states that the submissions are not audited before publication; use that information for market context, not as a substitute for a quote.

ctaBreaks.heading: See the recurring insurance cost inside the payment
ctaBreaks.body: Add the annual homeowners and flood estimates separately, then compare the monthly result with the deductible reserve you may need outside escrow.
ctaBreaks.label: Estimate a Florida mortgage payment

ctaBreaks.heading: Move from statewide guidance to a local market
ctaBreaks.body: Use Florida's location page to reach Tampa, Orlando, Miami, and other city-level mortgage planning resources.
ctaBreaks.label: Explore Florida mortgage markets

articles.conclusion: Florida insurance planning is not complete when a premium appears in the payment estimate. The policy must also show what is covered, which deductibles apply, how wind is handled, and whether flood protection is separate. Compare those terms while the property is still under review, then maintain both a monthly ownership budget and a realistic cash reserve for losses the homeowner must fund before coverage responds.

faqs.question: Is a Florida hurricane deductible based on the purchase price?
faqs.answer: Generally, a percentage hurricane deductible is tied to the policy's dwelling or structure limit, and the declarations page shows its dollar amount. Review the actual policy because options and exceptions vary.

faqs.question: Does homeowners insurance cover storm-surge or other flood damage?
faqs.answer: Most standard homeowners policies do not cover flood damage, and Florida's definition of hurricane coverage does not include flooding. Review separate flood-insurance options and the terms offered for the property.

faqs.question: Will escrow pay my insurance deductible after a loss?
faqs.answer: No. Escrow is generally used to collect and pay scheduled property expenses such as premiums and taxes. A claim deductible is the policyholder's responsibility and should be considered in a separate cash reserve.

sourceClaims.claim: Florida consumer guidance covers homeowners policy components, deductibles, inspections, endorsements, and the lender's potential insurance requirement.
Florida specifies the hurricane-deductible period, annual application rules, required dollar disclosure, available options, and exceptions; hurricane coverage does not include flooding.
Florida OIR market reports use insurer-submitted monthly county and ZIP-level data beginning in 2025, and OIR states that it does not audit the submissions before publication.
Most homeowners policies do not cover flood damage, and flood risk should be reviewed separately from standard property coverage.
An escrow account may collect insurance and tax costs monthly, and later changes in those bills can change the total mortgage payment.

articles.title: How FHA loans work for lower down payment buyers
articles.summary: FHA financing may help a buyer use a smaller down payment, but the useful comparison also includes mortgage insurance, county loan limits, property review, cash to close, and the cost over the years the loan may be kept.
articles.dek: An FHA label does not settle the decision. Compare an FHA scenario with other available loans using the same property, loan term, rate-lock period, and time horizon.
articles.metaDescription: Learn how FHA loans, mortgage insurance, county limits, property review, and closing costs fit into a careful home loan comparison.
introduction: An FHA loan is made by a private lender and insured through the Federal Housing Administration. That insurance may allow a lender to offer a lower-down-payment path than some conventional options, but it does not eliminate underwriting or turn a general program feature into an approval. The lender still reviews the borrower, property, occupancy, loan amount, and current FHA requirements.

The clearest way to evaluate FHA financing is to build a complete scenario. Start with the purchase price and planned down payment, confirm the current FHA limit for the property's county, and include both upfront and ongoing mortgage insurance in the comparison. Then place the FHA Loan Estimate beside any conventional or other program estimate that uses matching assumptions.
keyTakeaways: FHA insures loans made by approved private lenders; it does not lend money directly to the borrower.
A smaller down payment may preserve cash at closing while mortgage insurance and a larger balance may change monthly and long-run costs.
The applicable FHA loan limit depends on the property's county or supported metropolitan area and the current calendar-year table.
Use matched Loan Estimates to compare rate, APR, mortgage insurance, closing costs, cash to close, and total payment before choosing a path.

sections.heading: Understand what FHA insurance changes
paragraphs: FHA insurance protects the lender against certain losses; it is not insurance for the buyer's repairs, job loss, or future home value. Under HUD rules current as of July 2026, maximum purchase financing generally requires at least a 3.5% borrower investment when the FHA minimum decision credit score is 580 or higher. Scores from 500 through 579 are generally limited to 90% loan-to-value, and lenders may apply additional standards. The property, borrower, loan purpose, and full current handbook still require transaction-specific review.

For standard FHA forward mortgages, the current upfront mortgage insurance premium is generally 1.75% of the base loan amount. The annual premium is set by the loan amount, term, and loan-to-value ratio and is usually collected through monthly installments. Under the current schedule, annual mortgage insurance may remain for 11 years when the origination loan-to-value ratio is 90% or less and for the mortgage term when it is above 90%. Ask the lender to confirm the rate and assessment period for the proposed loan rather than relying on a general example.

The applicable upfront premium must generally be paid entirely in cash at closing or financed entirely into the mortgage, subject to HUD's rounding treatment and any transaction-specific exception. Financing reduces the immediate cash requirement but increases the balance on which interest is charged. Ask the lender to show both the base loan and total loan amount so the comparison does not hide that tradeoff.

sections.heading: Compare FHA and conventional scenarios on equal terms
paragraphs: A headline rate is not enough to identify the less expensive loan. Keep the property, price, occupancy, term, lock period, and expected closing date consistent, then compare the written offers line by line. If one scenario uses points or lender credits, request a second version without them or make the same pricing choice in the other scenario.

Include costs that sit outside principal and interest. Estimated property taxes, homeowners insurance, association dues, and mortgage insurance can materially change the housing payment. Cash to close should also remain separate from the monthly total so a low-cash structure is not mistaken for a low-cost loan.

table.caption: FHA comparison checklist
rows: Loan boundary
Base loan amount against the current FHA county limit
Property county, unit count, and current HUD lookup
Price of borrowing
Interest rate, APR, points, and lender credits
Same lock period and closing assumptions
Mortgage insurance

table.note: This checklist organizes a review; it does not state that an FHA or conventional loan is available to a particular borrower or property.

sections.heading: Check the property before relying on the program
paragraphs: FHA maximum mortgage amounts vary by location, so confirm the property's county rather than relying on a city name or an old national figure. Compare the proposed base loan amount with HUD's current lookup. Staying within the limit only clears one program boundary; it does not establish eligibility, value, or final loan terms.

The property also goes through an FHA appraisal and lender review under the current handbook. Ask early whether the home type, condition, utilities, planned repairs, or condominium status could require additional documentation. A buyer can separately consider an independent inspection and contract protections based on the home and local transaction practices.

sections.heading: Protect the budget beyond closing day
paragraphs: A lower initial down payment can leave more money available for moving, maintenance, and emergency reserves. It can also create a larger financed balance and a different insurance cost. Measure both sides: cash retained after closing and the projected payment under realistic tax, insurance, and association assumptions.

Before deciding, ask for written FHA and alternative scenarios from lenders who can offer them. Review the Loan Estimate's loan amount, projected payments, mortgage insurance, origination charges, lender credits, and cash to close. If future refinancing is part of the plan, treat it as an uncertain option rather than a promised exit from mortgage insurance or today's terms.

ctaBreaks.heading: See the FHA option in one place
ctaBreaks.body: Review the program's payment, mortgage-insurance, property, and documentation questions before deciding which scenarios to request.
ctaBreaks.label: Review FHA loan options

ctaBreaks.heading: Test the down payment tradeoff
ctaBreaks.body: Change the down payment while keeping the property and other assumptions steady to see how cash and the estimated payment move.
ctaBreaks.label: Compare down payment scenarios

articles.conclusion: FHA financing can be a useful path when its complete cost and property rules support the buyer's plan. The disciplined comparison is not FHA versus conventional in the abstract. It is one written FHA scenario beside other available offers for the same home, with mortgage insurance, cash to close, reserves, and the likely ownership period all visible.

faqs.question: Does a lower FHA down payment mean the loan costs less?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. A smaller down payment may preserve cash, but the resulting balance, mortgage insurance, rate, APR, fees, and time in the loan all affect the cost. Compare matched written estimates.

faqs.question: Does being under the FHA county limit mean I qualify?
faqs.answer: No. The limit is a loan-size boundary. A lender must still review income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, the property, and all current program requirements.

faqs.question: Can FHA mortgage insurance be removed later?
faqs.answer: The assessment period depends on the loan's characteristics and current HUD rules. Ask the lender to identify the applicable period in writing and do not assume a future refinance will be available.

sourceClaims.claim: CFPB explains that private lenders make FHA-insured loans, that FHA may support a lower down payment, that county limits apply, and that total costs should be compared with other options.
HUD Handbook 4000.1 provides the current FHA minimum-investment, credit-score, upfront and annual mortgage-insurance, assessment-period, appraisal, property, borrower, and lender requirements.
HUD's 2026 lookup provides FHA forward mortgage limits by county or supported metropolitan area; the limit is a program boundary rather than an approval.
CFPB's Loan Estimate explainer identifies the fields borrowers can use to compare loan terms, mortgage insurance, closing costs, lender credits, projected payments, and cash to close.

articles.title: VA loan basics for eligible Veterans, service members, and surviving spouses
articles.summary: A VA-backed loan can offer a distinct purchase path for eligible Veterans, service members, and some surviving spouses, but the Certificate of Eligibility, entitlement, lender review, property value, funding fee, and closing costs each answer a different question.
articles.dek: Start with the benefit record, then compare the actual loan. A COE documents VA home-loan eligibility based on service history; it does not promise a loan amount, rate, or approval.
articles.metaDescription: Understand VA loan COEs, entitlement, down payment options, funding fees, appraisals, closing costs, and lender review.
introduction: VA-backed purchase loans are issued by private lenders, while the Department of Veterans Affairs guarantees part of the loan. The first program step is usually confirming whether the borrower can receive a Certificate of Eligibility. The COE shows service-based benefit information and entitlement, but the lender separately reviews credit, income, debts, assets, occupancy, and the requested loan amount.

The program may permit a purchase without a down payment when the transaction, entitlement, appraisal, and lender review support that structure. That feature should be evaluated beside the VA funding fee, other closing costs, the amount financed, cash retained after closing, and the full monthly housing budget.
keyTakeaways: A Certificate of Eligibility confirms service-related VA home-loan benefit information; it does not replace the lender's underwriting decision.
A no-down-payment option may be available, but entitlement, appraised value, purchase price, lender standards, and the proposed loan all matter.
VA-backed loans do not require monthly mortgage insurance, while a one-time funding fee may apply unless the borrower has a confirmed exemption.
Interest rates, points, and many closing costs come from the private lender, so compare written offers rather than treating VA as a single price.

sections.heading: Separate the COE from loan approval
paragraphs: VA determines whether the service history and duty status support a COE. A borrower can request the certificate directly or may ask a lender to help retrieve it. Because eligibility rules vary by service period and circumstance, use the VA's current guidance rather than relying on a general service-length shortcut.

After the COE is available, the lender evaluates the financing request. The review may include documented income, debts, credit history, assets, occupancy, and the property's appraised value. Full entitlement does not mean an unlimited approval; it means the county loan limit does not cap the guaranty calculation in the same way it can when some entitlement is already in use.

sections.heading: Read entitlement, down payment, and value together
paragraphs: For a borrower with full entitlement, VA states that there is no home-loan limit as long as the borrower can afford the amount and the appraisal supports the purchase price. When entitlement is already in use, the county's FHFA conforming limit may affect the guaranty calculation, and a lender may require a down payment if remaining entitlement is not enough for the proposed loan.

A purchase price above the appraised value can create another cash decision. The buyer may discuss a reconsideration of value through the lender, renegotiate the contract, use an available contract right, or pay a difference in cash if the transaction can otherwise proceed. Those choices depend on the contract, available funds, and lender review.

table.caption: VA purchase review worksheet
rows: Certificate of Eligibility
Service-based benefit status and entitlement information
Current COE and any entitlement already charged
Down payment
Cash applied to the price
Whether entitlement, value, and lender terms support the proposed structure
Funding fee
Potential one-time program charge
Confirmed exemption status, applicable amount, and whether it is paid or financed
Monthly insurance
VA does not require monthly PMI or FHA-style MIP
Other payment components, including taxes and homeowners insurance
Appraisal

Contract options, repairs, and any separate home inspection
